title: Hypothermia
oneLiner: Accidental hypothermia: core temp <35°C; causes multi-organ dysfunction; mortality high if not recognised early.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ventricular fibrillation/cardiac arrest** (especially if core <28°C or during rough handling): single finding – loss of consciousness with bradycardia or irregular pulse; ECG may show J (Osborn) waves. **Do not** defibrillate more than 3 times until core temp >30°C.
- **Rewarming collapse** (rapid vasodilation → hypotension/pre-arrest): single finding – sudden drop in BP during active rewarming; always warn team before starting forced-air warming and have IV access + fluid ready.
- **Underlying reversible killer** (hypoglycaemia, sepsis, adrenal crisis, myxoedema coma): single finding – failure to rewarm or persistent hypotension despite adequate warming; check bedside glucose immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Shivering (absent below ~32°C) → confusion → lethargy → coma (GCS drops with severity)
  - Bradycardia, hypotension, slow respiratory rate
  - Cold, pale skin; muscle rigidity
  - Polyuria (cold diuresis) – may be missed if catheterised
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Core temp <35°C (low-reading rectal or oesophageal thermometer essential)
  - Poor peripheral pulses, delayed capillary refill
  - Stiff, rigid muscles (mimics Parkinson’s)
  - ECG: J wave (Osborn) at the junction of QRS-ST; prolonged QT; bradyarrhythmias
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Primary hypothermia** (environmental exposure) → clear history, no underlying illness, rewarms steadily
- **Secondary hypothermia** due to:
  - Sepsis (especially in elderly) – look for source, raised lactate, fever may be absent
  - Hypoglycaemia – <3.5 mmol/L, rapidly correctable
  - Myxoedema coma – bradycardia out of proportion, dry skin, history of hypothyroidism
  - Adrenal insufficiency – hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia
  - Drug overdose (alcohol, opiates, sedatives) – history, tox screen
- **Intracranial catastrophe** (stroke, subarachnoid haemorrhage) – focal neurology, unequal pupils, sudden onset
- **Splitter:** “Does the patient rewarm with passive measures alone?” – if not, suspect secondary cause (esp. sepsis or endocrine).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Core temperature (low-reading rectal thermometer; if <32°C use oesophageal/tympanic with low-range probe)
  - ECG – look for J wave, arrhythmias, signs of hyperkalaemia
  - Bedside glucose – must be done immediately (hypoglycaemia reversible)
  - Urine if possible – toxicology, urine output
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, glucose, blood gas (arterial or venous – note that corrected values for temperature are not needed acutely)
  - Thyroid function (TSH, T4) – if myxoedema suspected
  - Cortisol (random) and ACTH – if adrenal insufficiency suspected
  - Lactate – marker of shock
  - Toxicology screen (ethanol, paracetamol, salicylate, etc.)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR – look for infection, aspiration, pulmonary oedema
  - CT head – if focal neurology, trauma, or GCS <15
- **Special tests:** None routine.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Swiss staging (based on clinical + core temp):
    - Stage 1: conscious, shivering – temp 35–32°C
    - Stage 2: impaired consciousness, no shivering – temp 32–28°C
    - Stage 3: unconscious but vital signs present – temp 28–24°C
    - Stage 4: cardiac arrest – temp <24°C
- **Never-miss test:** **Bedside blood glucose** – hypoglycaemia can cause or complicate hypothermia and is rapidly correctable.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway, breathing, circulation – handle gently (rough handling precipitates VF)
  - Remove wet clothing, cover with passive insulation (blankets, space blanket)
  - Active external rewarming (forced-air warming) for mild-moderate (stage 1–2, temp >28°C)
  - Active internal rewarming for severe (stage 3–4): warmed IV fluids (40–42°C), warmed humidified oxygen; consider gastric/bladder/pleural lavage with warm saline; in cardiac arrest use ECMO/EMCO (if available)
  - Aim for rewarming rate of 0.5–1°C per hour (do not exceed 2°C/h externally without internal support)
  - **Do NOT** give repeated defibrillation if core <30°C – limit to 3 shocks; resume when core >30°C
  - **Do NOT** give IV vasopressors until core >30°C (ineffective, can cause arrhythmia)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor core temperature continuously
  - Re-warm slowly; avoid hyperthermia
  - Treat underlying cause (antibiotics for sepsis, thyroxine for myxoedema, hydrocortisone for adrenal crisis)
  - Monitor for complications: arrhythmia, pulmonary oedema, coagulopathy, rhabdomyolysis
  - Check electrolytes (K+, Ca²+, Mg²+) – hypocalcaemia can occur with rewarming
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If due to environmental exposure and no underlying cause, no further therapy
  - If secondary, arrange appropriate endocrine/medical follow-up
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: warmed (or room temperature if mild) crystalloid – for severe hypothermia use 1L warmed 0.9% saline over 1 hour, then reassess; avoid cold fluids
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin once core >35°C (may increase bleeding risk if coagulopathic)
  - Diet: NBM until GCS normal and able to protect airway (risk of aspiration)
  - Observations: continuous cardiac monitoring, hourly core temp, hourly neuro observations, NEWS2 (but note bradycardia may be physiological)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **No specific drug for hypothermia itself.** Treatment is supportive and targeted at underlying cause.
- **Antibiotics** (if sepsis) – choice depends on source; [check local policy]
- **Thyroxine (levothyroxine)** for myxoedema coma: [check local policy – typically 200–400 micrograms IV stat, then 50–100 micrograms daily; but do NOT give without senior endocrine advice]
- **Hydrocortisone** for adrenal crisis: 100 mg IV stat, then 50 mg IV every 6 hours
- **Vasopressors** – only after core >30°C: noradrenaline IV titrated to MAP >65 mmHg; start at low dose (e.g. 0.05 microgram/kg/min) [check local policy]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** All doses reduced in renal/hepatic impairment if applicable (e.g., antibiotics); hypothermia itself may reduce drug clearance.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Rewarming too quickly** – not a drug error but common management error; can cause fatal rewarming collapse. Also, **giving vasopressors before core >30°C** can precipitate VF.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Arrhythmias: atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopy, VF, asystole – especially during rewarming
  - Rewarming collapse (hypotension, pulmonary oedema)
  - Coagulopathy (due to cold → platelet dysfunction; may worsen with rewarming due to consumption)
  - Rhabdomyolysis (from shivering, cold muscles, or prolonged recumbency)
  - Hypoglycaemia (if underlying)
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurological deficits (cognitive, peripheral neuropathy) – more likely after prolonged severe hypothermia
  - Renal impairment (acute tubular necrosis from rhabdomyolysis or hypoperfusion)
sections.Prognosis: - Overall mortality ~30–80% depending on severity, underlying cause, and presence of cardiac arrest. Good prognostic factor if young, isolated environmental exposure, and no underlying illness. Poor prognostic factor if core <28°C with cardiac arrest, or if secondary to sepsis.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document core temperature with a low-reading thermometer** – standard tympanic/devices may not read below 34°C.
- **Suspect underlying sepsis or endocrine cause in any hypothermic patient who fails to rewarm with passive measures** – do not delay blood cultures, cortisol, and TSH.
- **If patient arrests, continue resuscitation until core >35°C** – the only situation where prolonged CPR is indicated (survivable even after >60 minutes).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not handle the patient roughly** – rough movement can precipitate VF in unstable hearts.
- **Do not delay defibrillation if VF persists despite core <30°C** – still give up to 3 shocks, but focus on rewarming first.
- **Do not use cool or room temperature IV fluids** – worsens hypothermia; always warm fluids if core <35°C.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** confusion or drowsiness recurs, severe shivering, chest pain/palpitations, reduced urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/h), or inability to maintain warmth in a normal environment.
- **Follow-up:** If primary cause (e.g., exposure) and fully resolved, no specific follow-up needed. If secondary cause (e.g., endocrine, sepsis) – arrange GP or specialist follow-up within 1 week.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/Medical Registrar if:**
  - Core temp <32°C (even if stable) or fails to rise with active measures after 1 hour
  - GCS <9
  - Any new arrhythmia (esp. VF, asystole, complete heart block)
  - Haemodynamic instability (MAP <65 mmHg or lactate >4 mmol/L)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (with caveat that bradycardia may be physiological; look for other triggers)
  - Signs of rewarming collapse (sudden hypotension)
- **Call ICU if:** core temp <28°C, cardiac arrest, or requiring internal rewarming (ECMO/ECPR)

rawMarkdown:
## Hypothermia  
**One-liner:** Accidental hypothermia: core temp <35°C; causes multi-organ dysfunction; mortality high if not recognised early.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ventricular fibrillation/cardiac arrest** (especially if core <28°C or during rough handling): single finding – loss of consciousness with bradycardia or irregular pulse; ECG may show J (Osborn) waves. **Do not** defibrillate more than 3 times until core temp >30°C.
- **Rewarming collapse** (rapid vasodilation → hypotension/pre-arrest): single finding – sudden drop in BP during active rewarming; always warn team before starting forced-air warming and have IV access + fluid ready.
- **Underlying reversible killer** (hypoglycaemia, sepsis, adrenal crisis, myxoedema coma): single finding – failure to rewarm or persistent hypotension despite adequate warming; check bedside glucose immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Shivering (absent below ~32°C) → confusion → lethargy → coma (GCS drops with severity)
  - Bradycardia, hypotension, slow respiratory rate
  - Cold, pale skin; muscle rigidity
  - Polyuria (cold diuresis) – may be missed if catheterised
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Core temp <35°C (low-reading rectal or oesophageal thermometer essential)
  - Poor peripheral pulses, delayed capillary refill
  - Stiff, rigid muscles (mimics Parkinson’s)
  - ECG: J wave (Osborn) at the junction of QRS-ST; prolonged QT; bradyarrhythmias

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Primary hypothermia** (environmental exposure) → clear history, no underlying illness, rewarms steadily
- **Secondary hypothermia** due to:
  - Sepsis (especially in elderly) – look for source, raised lactate, fever may be absent
  - Hypoglycaemia – <3.5 mmol/L, rapidly correctable
  - Myxoedema coma – bradycardia out of proportion, dry skin, history of hypothyroidism
  - Adrenal insufficiency – hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia
  - Drug overdose (alcohol, opiates, sedatives) – history, tox screen
- **Intracranial catastrophe** (stroke, subarachnoid haemorrhage) – focal neurology, unequal pupils, sudden onset
- **Splitter:** “Does the patient rewarm with passive measures alone?” – if not, suspect secondary cause (esp. sepsis or endocrine).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Core temperature (low-reading rectal thermometer; if <32°C use oesophageal/tympanic with low-range probe)
  - ECG – look for J wave, arrhythmias, signs of hyperkalaemia
  - Bedside glucose – must be done immediately (hypoglycaemia reversible)
  - Urine if possible – toxicology, urine output
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, glucose, blood gas (arterial or venous – note that corrected values for temperature are not needed acutely)
  - Thyroid function (TSH, T4) – if myxoedema suspected
  - Cortisol (random) and ACTH – if adrenal insufficiency suspected
  - Lactate – marker of shock
  - Toxicology screen (ethanol, paracetamol, salicylate, etc.)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR – look for infection, aspiration, pulmonary oedema
  - CT head – if focal neurology, trauma, or GCS <15
- **Special tests:** None routine.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Swiss staging (based on clinical + core temp):
    - Stage 1: conscious, shivering – temp 35–32°C
    - Stage 2: impaired consciousness, no shivering – temp 32–28°C
    - Stage 3: unconscious but vital signs present – temp 28–24°C
    - Stage 4: cardiac arrest – temp <24°C
- **Never-miss test:** **Bedside blood glucose** – hypoglycaemia can cause or complicate hypothermia and is rapidly correctable.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway, breathing, circulation – handle gently (rough handling precipitates VF)
  - Remove wet clothing, cover with passive insulation (blankets, space blanket)
  - Active external rewarming (forced-air warming) for mild-moderate (stage 1–2, temp >28°C)
  - Active internal rewarming for severe (stage 3–4): warmed IV fluids (40–42°C), warmed humidified oxygen; consider gastric/bladder/pleural lavage with warm saline; in cardiac arrest use ECMO/EMCO (if available)
  - Aim for rewarming rate of 0.5–1°C per hour (do not exceed 2°C/h externally without internal support)
  - **Do NOT** give repeated defibrillation if core <30°C – limit to 3 shocks; resume when core >30°C
  - **Do NOT** give IV vasopressors until core >30°C (ineffective, can cause arrhythmia)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor core temperature continuously
  - Re-warm slowly; avoid hyperthermia
  - Treat underlying cause (antibiotics for sepsis, thyroxine for myxoedema, hydrocortisone for adrenal crisis)
  - Monitor for complications: arrhythmia, pulmonary oedema, coagulopathy, rhabdomyolysis
  - Check electrolytes (K+, Ca²+, Mg²+) – hypocalcaemia can occur with rewarming
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If due to environmental exposure and no underlying cause, no further therapy
  - If secondary, arrange appropriate endocrine/medical follow-up
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: warmed (or room temperature if mild) crystalloid – for severe hypothermia use 1L warmed 0.9% saline over 1 hour, then reassess; avoid cold fluids
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin once core >35°C (may increase bleeding risk if coagulopathic)
  - Diet: NBM until GCS normal and able to protect airway (risk of aspiration)
  - Observations: continuous cardiac monitoring, hourly core temp, hourly neuro observations, NEWS2 (but note bradycardia may be physiological)

### Drugs & Doses
- **No specific drug for hypothermia itself.** Treatment is supportive and targeted at underlying cause.
- **Antibiotics** (if sepsis) – choice depends on source; [check local policy]
- **Thyroxine (levothyroxine)** for myxoedema coma: [check local policy – typically 200–400 micrograms IV stat, then 50–100 micrograms daily; but do NOT give without senior endocrine advice]
- **Hydrocortisone** for adrenal crisis: 100 mg IV stat, then 50 mg IV every 6 hours
- **Vasopressors** – only after core >30°C: noradrenaline IV titrated to MAP >65 mmHg; start at low dose (e.g. 0.05 microgram/kg/min) [check local policy]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** All doses reduced in renal/hepatic impairment if applicable (e.g., antibiotics); hypothermia itself may reduce drug clearance.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Rewarming too quickly** – not a drug error but common management error; can cause fatal rewarming collapse. Also, **giving vasopressors before core >30°C** can precipitate VF.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Arrhythmias: atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopy, VF, asystole – especially during rewarming
  - Rewarming collapse (hypotension, pulmonary oedema)
  - Coagulopathy (due to cold → platelet dysfunction; may worsen with rewarming due to consumption)
  - Rhabdomyolysis (from shivering, cold muscles, or prolonged recumbency)
  - Hypoglycaemia (if underlying)
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurological deficits (cognitive, peripheral neuropathy) – more likely after prolonged severe hypothermia
  - Renal impairment (acute tubular necrosis from rhabdomyolysis or hypoperfusion)

### Prognosis
- Overall mortality ~30–80% depending on severity, underlying cause, and presence of cardiac arrest. Good prognostic factor if young, isolated environmental exposure, and no underlying illness. Poor prognostic factor if core <28°C with cardiac arrest, or if secondary to sepsis.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document core temperature with a low-reading thermometer** – standard tympanic/devices may not read below 34°C.
- **Suspect underlying sepsis or endocrine cause in any hypothermic patient who fails to rewarm with passive measures** – do not delay blood cultures, cortisol, and TSH.
- **If patient arrests, continue resuscitation until core >35°C** – the only situation where prolonged CPR is indicated (survivable even after >60 minutes).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not handle the patient roughly** – rough movement can precipitate VF in unstable hearts.
- **Do not delay defibrillation if VF persists despite core <30°C** – still give up to 3 shocks, but focus on rewarming first.
- **Do not use cool or room temperature IV fluids** – worsens hypothermia; always warm fluids if core <35°C.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** confusion or drowsiness recurs, severe shivering, chest pain/palpitations, reduced urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/h), or inability to maintain warmth in a normal environment.
- **Follow-up:** If primary cause (e.g., exposure) and fully resolved, no specific follow-up needed. If secondary cause (e.g., endocrine, sepsis) – arrange GP or specialist follow-up within 1 week.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/Medical Registrar if:**
  - Core temp <32°C (even if stable) or fails to rise with active measures after 1 hour
  - GCS <9
  - Any new arrhythmia (esp. VF, asystole, complete heart block)
  - Haemodynamic instability (MAP <65 mmHg or lactate >4 mmol/L)
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (with caveat that bradycardia may be physiological; look for other triggers)
  - Signs of rewarming collapse (sudden hypotension)
- **Call ICU if:** core temp <28°C, cardiac arrest, or requiring internal rewarming (ECMO/ECPR)
